policy_statement: Helen will implement customer-end emissions reduction actions including improving district heating connection measurements and tariff adjustments, examining hourly district heating tariffs, and developing energy monitoring interfaces for customers.
mechanism_description: Helen will improve district heating connection measurements, adjust (and examine hourly) tariffs, and develop customer energy monitoring interfaces to create better information and price incentives that reduce district heating energy use and related emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: improving district heating connection measurements and tariff adjustments, examining hourly district heating tariffs, and developing energy monitoring interfaces for customers
primary_causal_pathway: Information + price signals → ↓ heat demand → ↓ fuel combustion for heat production → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Improve metering accuracy/granularity, introduce hourly/dynamic tariffs, and provide customer energy monitoring interfaces to influence end-user heat consumption and enable better control/awareness.  
(2) Mediating process: Price elasticity and feedback effects drive conservation and load shifting; lower aggregate heat load reduces heat generation set and upstream fuel input (and may improve plant dispatch/efficiency).  
(3) Outcome: Reduced combustion-related CO₂ (and potentially CH₄/N₂O depending on fuel mix) from district heating production, yielding net GHG mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced district heating demand, which lowers fuel combustion at heat plants and therefore reduces GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Metering + dynamic pricing changes consumption behavior → lower district heat demand → less heat generation and fuel combustion → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism can improve energy productivity (a resource-efficiency framing), the climate-relevant endpoint emphasized and most consequential is the reduction in combustion emissions from heat production, which is mitigation. It is not primarily adaptation (no hazard/exposure pathway) or nature-based solutions (no ecosystem sink pathway).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the district heating system were already near-zero-carbon (e.g., fully renewable/waste heat with minimal marginal emissions), the dominant climate effect could shift toward resource efficiency/cost savings; however, for the mechanism as defined (“related emissions” reduction from lower heat use), the general dominant pathway remains emissions mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses metering + dynamic pricing to reduce district heat demand will, in typical systems, reduce required heat generation and thus GHG emissions; therefore Mitigation is the consistent primary category for this canonical mechanism.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: The exact mitigation subtype (e.g., demand-side efficiency vs. demand response vs. pricing reform) and quantified impact depend on policy-specific evidence (fuel mix, marginal emissions, tariff structure), and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If marginal district heat is supplied by low/zero-carbon sources (e.g., surplus renewable heat, geothermal, large heat pumps on zero-carbon electricity, or waste heat that would otherwise be rejected), the emissions reduction per unit of heat saved may be small and the dominant framing could lean toward Resource Efficiency; nevertheless the canonical mechanism’s intended and typical climate impact is mitigation via reduced heat production emissions.
key_indicators: “changing the tariffs”; “hourly tariff for district heating will be examined”; “incentive effects”; “inspection of the measurements”; “monitoring energy consumption”; “offering the client an open interface”
co_benefits: Improved billing accuracy and fairness; potential customer cost savings; better demand management and peak load reduction; improved customer engagement/service quality.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both pricing (tariffs) and information/feedback (monitoring interface). Classified by the primary lever (tariff design) as a pricing mechanism; the interface component could alternatively be coded as information-disclosure if treated as the main instrument.
